question: What is Depo-Provera?
answer: Depo-Provera is a birth control shot (injection) that women get every 3 months to prevent pregnancy. It contains a hormone called medroxyprogesterone acetate. It has been available in the United States since 1992 and was widely used at clinics like Planned Parenthood. A generic version called depot medroxyprogesterone acetate (DMPA) also exists — if you got that shot, you may qualify too.

question: How does the Depo shot cause brain tumors?
answer: Research shows that the hormone in the Depo shot can cause a type of brain tumor called a meningioma to grow. A large study published in the British Medical Journal found that women who used Depo-Provera for more than one year were 5 times more likely to develop a meningioma than women who did not use it. The longer a woman used it, the higher the risk.

question: Who can file a Depo-Provera lawsuit?
answer: You may be able to file a lawsuit if: (1) you received Depo-Provera or a generic depot medroxyprogesterone shot between 1992 and 2019; (2) you used it for at least 1 year (4 or more shots); (3) you were later diagnosed with a meningioma (brain tumor); and (4) you are not already working with a lawyer on this case. You do not need to have had surgery to qualify — even a confirmed diagnosis from an MRI or CT scan may be enough.

question: Why does it matter if I used it between 1992 and 2019?
answer: Pfizer did not add a brain tumor warning to Depo-Provera until 2023. Women who used it before 2019 were never warned about this risk. That is the basis of the lawsuit — Pfizer knew or should have known about the danger and chose not to warn patients. If you used it after the warning was added, your case may be harder to prove.

question: What is a meningioma — and do I need surgery to qualify?
answer: A meningioma is a tumor that grows in the membranes around the brain. Many are slow-growing and not cancerous, but they can still press on the brain and cause serious problems like headaches, vision loss, and memory issues. You do NOT need to have had surgery to qualify for a lawsuit. A confirmed diagnosis from a brain scan (MRI or CT) is enough to get started.

question: What if I used a generic version of the shot?
answer: Generic versions of Depo-Provera — sometimes called depot medroxyprogesterone acetate or DMPA — may also qualify. The active ingredient is the same. If you are not sure what brand you received, your doctor's office or pharmacy records can help confirm it.

question: What money could I receive?
answer: Women who qualify may be able to get money for: medical bills (MRIs, surgery, radiation, follow-up care), lost wages if the tumor kept you from working, pain and suffering, and emotional distress. The amount depends on how serious your diagnosis is and how long you used Depo-Provera. Every case is different — a lawyer can give you a better idea after reviewing your specific situation.

question: How long do I have to file?
answer: Every state has a deadline for filing — usually 1 to 3 years from when you were diagnosed or when you first learned that Depo-Provera may have caused your tumor. These deadlines are strict. If you miss it, you may lose your right to get any money. That is why it is important to check your eligibility now, even if you were diagnosed a while ago.

question: How much does a lawyer cost?
answer: Nothing upfront. Lawyers who handle these cases work on what is called a contingency fee — meaning they only get paid if you win or settle your case. Your first review is completely free and there is no obligation to move forward. You have nothing to lose by checking.
